evidence_points.quote: For adults with sudden-onset dizziness and a focal neurological deficit... refer immediately to exclude posterior circulation stroke.
For sudden-onset acute vestibular syndrome, HINTS should be performed if trained clinician available; stroke-pattern HINTS findings require immediate neuroimaging/referral.
If HINTS-trained clinician not available and BPPV/postural hypotension do not account for presentation, refer immediately per local stroke pathways.
evidence_points.threshold_type: Default emergency pathway when uncertainty remains

evidence_points.quote: For transient rotational vertigo on head movement: offer Hallpike; if BPPV diagnosed offer canalith repositioning (e.g. Epley) if trained clinician available.
Most people with vertigo do not need secondary care assessment. If vestibular apparatus lesion suspected, ENT is the best route.
Vestibular sedatives should only be used for acute vestibular syndrome; long-term use is not recommended.
BPPV episodes are typically <30 seconds positional rotational vertigo; positive Dix-Hallpike with torsional nystagmus ~30 seconds.
If primary care manoeuvres fail, routine referral to Audiology; advise Brandt-Daroff/Cawthorne-Cooksey while awaiting appointment.
Routine blood tests and imaging are rarely helpful in isolated vertigo; Hallpike is the most useful investigation in primary care.
Phone 999/A&E for vertigo with double vision, hearing loss, difficulty/slurred speech, limb weakness/numbness.
If symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks, referral to vestibular physiotherapy or ENT may be required.
ABN A&G FAQ recommends use of local dizziness pathways (e.g., NHS Lothian) and NICE NG127 for dizziness/vertigo queries.
